content.problemStatement:
An array AAA consisting of 2⋅N2 \cdot N2⋅N elements is said to be **good** if it satisfies the following properties:


1≤Ai≤N1 \le A_i \le N1≤Ai​≤N for all 1≤i≤2⋅N1 \le i \le 2 \cdot N1≤i≤2⋅N
A2⋅i−1=A2⋅iA_{2 \cdot i - 1} = A_{2 \cdot i}A2⋅i−1​=A2⋅i​ for all 1≤i≤N1 \le i \le N1≤i≤N.
A2⋅i≠A2⋅jA_{2 \cdot i} \ne A_{2 \cdot j}A2⋅i​=A2⋅j​ for all 1≤i<j≤N1 \le i < j \le N1≤i<j≤N.
For example, some good arrays are [2,2,1,1][2, 2, 1, 1][2,2,1,1] and [2,2,1,1,3,3][2, 2, 1, 1, 3, 3][2,2,1,1,3,3].

You are given an array AAA which may not be good. You want to find the **minimum distance** to some good array BBB.

The distance between 222 arrays CCC and DDD (∣C∣=∣D∣|C| = |D|∣C∣=∣D∣) is the number of indices iii such that 1≤i≤∣C∣1 \le i \le |C|1≤i≤∣C∣ and Ci≠DiC_i \ne D_iCi​=Di​.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains NNN.
The second line contains 2⋅N2 \cdot N2⋅N integers - A1,A2,…,A2⋅NA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_{2 \cdot N}A1​,A2​,…,A2⋅N​.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the minimum distance to a good array.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 2⋅1052 \cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 1: The array is already good.
Test case 2: One example of a good array BBB is [1,1,2,2,3,3][1, 1, 2, 2, 3, 3][1,1,2,2,3,3] which has a distance of 444.
